items.subject: psychology
items.topic: the peak-end rule in how people remember experiences
items.transcript: Think back to the last vacation you took. When you remember it now, do you replay every single hour? Probably not. Your memory of the whole trip is shaped by just a couple of moments. This brings us to a concept in psychology called the peak-end rule. The idea is that when we judge a past experience, we don't average out every moment of it. Instead, our memory leans heavily on two things: the most intense moment, the peak, and how the experience ended. The boring middle stretches mostly fade away. Let me give you a well-known study. The psychologist Daniel Kahneman looked at patients undergoing an uncomfortable medical procedure. One group had a shorter procedure that ended at a painful moment. A second group had a longer procedure, with the same painful peak, but the final minutes were made deliberately milder. Now, logically, the second group endured more total discomfort, because their procedure lasted longer. Yet afterward, they remembered the whole experience as less unpleasant. The gentler ending reshaped the entire memory. So what does this tell us? It suggests that our remembering self and our experiencing self are not the same. The version of us that lives through an event minute by minute is not the version that later decides whether it was good or bad. This has real consequences. It means a trip with one spectacular afternoon and a smooth final day may be remembered more fondly than a uniformly pleasant but unremarkable one. Designers, doctors, even teachers can use this. End on a high note, and the whole thing is remembered better.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How memories of an experience are shaped mainly by its peak and ending
options.B: Why patients undergoing medical procedures feel less pain over time
options.C: How Daniel Kahneman designed his studies on human discomfort
options.D: Why vacations are remembered more fondly than ordinary days

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心是峰终定律：记忆主要由最强烈时刻（峰值）和结尾决定，故 A 正确。B 把'记忆感受较轻'误解为'疼痛减少'，且只是一个例子；C 只是细节，过窄；D 只是结尾的一个应用举例，并非主旨。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what was different about the second group in Kahneman's study?

options.A: Their procedure skipped the most painful peak entirely
options.B: Their procedure lasted longer but ended with milder final minutes
options.C: Their procedure was shorter and ended at a painful moment
options.D: Their procedure caused less total discomfort than the first group's

questions.explanation: 讲座说第二组手术更长，但最后几分钟被故意做得更温和，故 B 对。A 错，两组都有相同的疼痛峰值；C 描述的是第一组；D 与讲座相反，第二组总不适其实更多。

questions.stem: Why does the professor distinguish between the 'remembering self' and the 'experiencing self'?

options.A: To recommend that people keep detailed diaries of every event
options.B: To prove that medical procedures should always be made shorter
options.C: To explain why a longer, more painful event can be recalled as better
options.D: To describe the two groups of patients who took part in the study

questions.explanation: 教授引入'记忆中的自我'与'体验中的自我'的区分，是为了解释为何更长、更痛的经历反而被记得更好，故 C 对。A 凭空发挥；B 把结论夸大为应缩短手术；D 只是复述研究内容，未解释目的。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how to make an experience memorable?

options.A: Every moment of an experience should be kept equally pleasant
options.B: Long experiences are always remembered better than short ones
options.C: Painful experiences cannot be remembered in a positive way
options.D: Arranging a strong high point and a good ending improves the memory

questions.explanation: 讲座结尾说'End on a high note'并提到峰值，可推出安排好强烈高点和良好结尾能改善记忆，故 D 对。A 与峰终定律相反，并非每刻都要愉快；B 过度概括，时长不是关键；C 与研究结论相反，痛苦经历也能被记成较好。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how coral reefs respond to ocean acidification
items.transcript: Coral reefs are often called the rainforests of the sea, and like rainforests, they're under serious pressure. Today I want to focus on one threat that's less visible than warming water, and that's ocean acidification. Here's the chemistry. The ocean absorbs roughly a quarter of the carbon dioxide we release into the atmosphere. When carbon dioxide dissolves in seawater, it forms carbonic acid, and this lowers the water's pH. Now, why does that matter for coral? Corals build their stony skeletons out of calcium carbonate, the same material as chalk. To do that, they need carbonate ions dissolved in the water. But as the water becomes more acidic, those carbonate ions become scarcer, because the extra acid ties them up. So the coral has to spend more energy just to pull together the building blocks it needs. The result isn't always dramatic bleaching. Often it's subtler. Reefs grow more slowly, their skeletons become less dense, and they're more easily eroded by storms and grazing fish. One long-term study on the Great Barrier Reef found that calcification rates dropped notably over recent decades. And here's a complication researchers have noticed. Acidification rarely acts alone. It usually arrives together with warmer water, and the two stresses compound each other. A coral already weakened by heat copes even worse with acidic conditions. That's why predicting reef futures is so hard. You can't simply add up the effects. So the picture emerging from current research is one of gradual decline rather than sudden collapse, which, in some ways, makes it easier to overlook until the damage is severe.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why warming water is the single greatest threat to coral reefs
options.B: How ocean acidification makes it harder for corals to build skeletons
options.C: How carbon dioxide is absorbed and stored in the deep ocean
options.D: Why coral bleaching events have become more dramatic recently

questions.explanation: 讲座主要讲海洋酸化如何使珊瑚更难建造碳酸钙骨骼，故 B 对。A 与讲座相反，重点是酸化这个'不太显眼'的威胁而非升温；C 只是解释化学背景的细节，过窄；D 与讲座相反，讲座强调影响往往不是剧烈白化而是渐进衰退。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do carbonate ions become scarcer as seawater grows more acidic?

options.A: Because warmer water dissolves the coral skeletons directly
options.B: Because grazing fish consume the carbonate before corals can use it
options.C: Because the extra acid ties up the available carbonate ions
options.D: Because corals stop absorbing carbonate once bleaching begins

questions.explanation: 讲座说'the extra acid ties them up'，即多余的酸把碳酸根离子结合住，故 C 对。A 混淆了升温这一独立因素；B 凭空发挥，啃食鱼是侵蚀骨骼而非消耗离子；D 与文意无关，是编造。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the long-term study on the Great Barrier Reef find?

options.A: Coral skeletons became denser and more storm-resistant over time
options.B: Bleaching events disappeared once acidification was measured
options.C: Warmer water alone fully explained the reef's recent decline
options.D: Calcification rates dropped notably over recent decades

questions.explanation: 讲座原话指出该研究发现'calcification rates dropped notably over recent decades'，故 D 对。A 与文意相反，骨骼变得不那么致密；B 凭空发挥；C 与讲座相反，讲座强调酸化与升温叠加，不能只归因于升温。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the combined effects of acidification and warming?

options.A: Their combined harm cannot be predicted by simply adding each effect
options.B: Acidification matters only when water temperatures stay constant
options.C: Warming protects corals from the worst impacts of acidification
options.D: The two stresses affect reefs in completely separate regions

questions.explanation: 讲座说两种压力'compound each other'且'You can't simply add up the effects'，可推出其叠加危害无法用简单相加来预测，故 A 对。B 与讲座相反，酸化常与升温同时出现；C 与文意相反，升温会让珊瑚更难应对酸化；D 凭空发挥。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: the role of patronage in shaping Baroque art
items.transcript: When we admire a dramatic Baroque painting today, we tend to credit the genius of the artist alone. But behind almost every major Baroque work stood a patron, and patronage shaped this art far more than people realize. Let me explain what patronage means here. A patron was a wealthy or powerful sponsor, often a church, a monarch, or a noble family, who paid the artist and, crucially, dictated much of what got made. In the seventeenth century, the Catholic Church was the dominant patron. After the Reformation, the Church wanted art that would move ordinary worshippers emotionally and draw them back to the faith. So it commissioned paintings full of drama, deep shadow, and intense feeling. Consider Caravaggio. His religious scenes use stark contrasts of light and dark to make sacred figures look startlingly real and immediate. That style wasn't just personal taste. It served the Church's goal of emotional persuasion. Now, patronage also meant constraints. An artist couldn't simply paint whatever he wished. The patron specified the subject, the size, sometimes even which saints to include and where. A contract might fix the deadline and the exact pigments to be used. And yet, within those limits, the most gifted artists found room to innovate. Bernini, working for popes, transformed sculpture and architecture while still serving his patrons' aims. So here's the larger point. Baroque art was the product of a partnership, and often a negotiation, between the vision of the artist and the demands of the powerful people paying for it. To understand the art, you really have to understand who was funding it and why.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Caravaggio invented the use of light and dark in painting
options.B: Why the Catholic Church lost power during the Reformation
options.C: How patrons shaped the subjects and style of Baroque art
options.D: Why Baroque sculpture was more advanced than its painting

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是赞助人如何塑造巴洛克艺术的主题与风格，故 C 对。A 只是一个例子，过窄；B 是讲座提到的背景而非主题，且讲座说宗教改革后教会仍是主导赞助人；D 凭空比较，讲座未作此对比。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what kind of art did the Catholic Church want after the Reformation?

options.A: Calm, simple works that avoided any religious subjects
options.B: Abstract designs meant mainly for private noble collections
options.C: Small portraits commissioned by individual worshippers
options.D: Dramatic, emotional works that drew worshippers back to the faith

questions.explanation: 讲座说教会想要充满戏剧、深影和强烈情感、能打动信徒并把他们拉回信仰的艺术，故 D 对。A 与文意相反；B 凭空发挥，且赞助主体是教会而非贵族私藏；C 与讲座相反，赞助人是教会而非个别信徒。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the details a contract might specify, such as deadlines and pigments?

options.A: To illustrate how much control patrons had over the artist's work
options.B: To argue that Baroque artists had no creative freedom at all
options.C: To explain how artists chose their own materials and schedules
options.D: To show that contracts were rarely honored by wealthy patrons

questions.explanation: 教授列举合同细节是为了说明赞助人对作品的控制之深，故 A 对。B 过度发挥，讲座明确说艺术家仍能在限制内创新；C 与文意相反，是赞助人而非艺术家决定；D 凭空发挥，讲座未提合同是否被遵守。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about understanding a Baroque artwork?

options.A: It can be understood by studying the artist's biography alone
options.B: It requires knowing who funded the work and for what purpose
options.C: It depends only on identifying which pigments were used
options.D: It is impossible unless the original contract still survives

questions.explanation: 讲座结尾说要理解这门艺术'you really have to understand who was funding it and why'，故 B 对。A 与讲座相反，开篇就批评只归功于艺术家；C 把一个细节当作全部，过窄；D 过度发挥，讲座未说必须有合同存世。

items.subject: environmental_science
items.topic: how green roofs reduce urban stormwater runoff
items.transcript: Picture a heavy rainstorm hitting a typical city. The water lands on streets, sidewalks, and conventional rooftops, surfaces that absorb almost nothing. So it rushes into drains all at once, often overwhelming the system and causing flooding. Today I want to talk about one increasingly popular solution, and that's green roofs. A green roof is exactly what it sounds like: a rooftop covered with a layer of soil and living plants, rather than bare tar or gravel. Now, how does that help with stormwater? It comes down to a few mechanisms. First, the soil and plants soak up rainwater like a sponge, holding a good portion of it instead of letting it run straight off. Second, some of that stored water is later released slowly back into the air through evaporation and through the plants themselves, a process called evapotranspiration. And third, even the water that does eventually drain off leaves the roof more gradually, spread out over hours instead of minutes. That delay is crucial, because it means the drains never face one giant surge. Studies in several cities have measured the effect. A well-designed green roof can retain something like half of the annual rainfall that lands on it, sometimes more in lighter storms. There are added bonuses too. Green roofs cool the building beneath them and provide small patches of habitat for insects and birds. But the stormwater benefit is often what convinces a city to offer incentives for installing them. So as cities grow denser and storms grow more intense, these living roofs are becoming a practical piece of urban water management.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why conventional city rooftops are cheaper than green roofs
options.B: How insects and birds benefit from new urban habitats
options.C: Why city drainage systems flood during heavy rainstorms
options.D: How green roofs reduce stormwater runoff in cities

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是绿色屋顶如何减少城市雨水径流，故 D 对。A 凭空比较成本，讲座未谈；B 只是附带好处之一，过窄；C 只是引出问题的背景，并非主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is one way a green roof handles rainwater?

options.A: Its soil and plants soak up and hold a portion of the rain
options.B: It channels all the rainfall directly into the city drains at once
options.C: It freezes the rainwater so it cannot reach the drains below
options.D: It pumps the rainwater down into the building's basement

questions.explanation: 讲座说土壤和植物像海绵一样吸收并保留一部分雨水，故 A 对。B 与文意相反，绿色屋顶正是为了避免雨水一次性涌入排水系统；C、D 都是凭空发挥，讲座从未提及。

questions.stem: Why does the professor explain that drained water leaves a green roof gradually over hours?

options.A: To suggest that green roofs eliminate the need for any drains
options.B: To explain why the delay keeps drains from facing one giant surge
options.C: To describe how evapotranspiration moves water into the air
options.D: To prove that green roofs work only during very light storms

questions.explanation: 教授强调排水缓慢分散在数小时内，是为了说明这种延迟让排水系统不必应对一次巨大的水流冲击，故 B 对。A 过度发挥，讲座未说可省去排水管；C 是另一个机制，混淆了内容；D 与讲座相反，讲座说较小雨时保留更多但并非只在小雨有效。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about why cities offer incentives for green roofs?

options.A: Cities mainly want to create habitat for insects and birds
options.B: Cities believe green roofs will fully replace traditional drains
options.C: Cities value the stormwater benefit most when deciding to encourage them
options.D: Cities install green roofs chiefly to cool the buildings beneath them

questions.explanation: 讲座说雨水管理的好处'is often what convinces a city to offer incentives'，可推出城市最看重的是雨水效益，故 C 对。A、D 是讲座所说的'附带好处'，并非促使提供激励的主因；B 过度发挥，讲座从未说能完全取代排水管。
